EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved from the gulf coast to offshore the Carolinas and up the east coast from the 18th to the 19th. This storm produced heavy snow across Garrett county Maryland, portions of northern West Virginia, and portions of Fayette and Westmorland counties in Pennsylvania. Total storm accumulations were from one to 2 feet in Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia with over a foot in Garrett county Maryland. Storm totals were 6 to 12 inches elsewhere.
An area of low pressure moving off the Carolina coast produced heavy snow in the ridges of northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland with storm totals from 6 to 8 inches in 12 hours.
Coastal low pressure produced between four and eleven inches of snow across portions of Lower Maryland from late Sunday afternoon, March 1st, into Monday morning March 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between four and eleven inches across the county. Church Creek reported 11.0 inches of snow. Cambridge reported 6.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As warm air headed north ahead of low pressure moving into the Ohio Valley freezing rain quickly developed across much of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Untreated roadways quickly accumulated up to one tenth of an inch of ice making travel nearly impossible between 6 AM and 10 AM. Major interstates in and around Pittsburgh, including the Pennsylvania Turnpike were closed for more than 2 hours until roads could be treated and surface temperatures warmed above freezing. 911 centers across the area received thousands of call due to minor accidents. 2 more serious accidents resulted in 2 deaths, one in Beaver county, PA and one in Westmoreland county, PA.

A persistent onshore flow developed around low pressure off the North Carolina Coast and high pressure over New England on the 12th into the 13th. This caused water levels to rise into a couple streets right along the Chesapeake Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal flooding was responsible for road closures in Solomons, Maryland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the Tennessee Valley and emerged off the Mid-Atlantic coast on the 5th. This system spread precipitation across Maryland. There was enough cold air drawn in from the north for precipitation to fall in the form of snow across most areas. Most of the snow fell during the morning and afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 2.5 inches of snow fell near Bryans Road while 2.0 inches of snow fell in Waldorf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked up through the Great Lakes. Plenty of moisture from the Atlantic Ocean and Gulf of Mexico was drawn into this system to produce widespread precipitation. There was enough cold air at the start of the storm for mixed precipitation, but warmer air did eventually work its way into the storm changing all mixed precipitation to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet accumulations were reported to be around 5 inches in Mount Savage and Eckhart Mines. Ice accumulation from freezing rain was just over one tenth of an inch at Ekhart Mines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air moved over the Upper Ohio Valley with a large dome of High pressure. Morning lows across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. The lowest low temperatures ranged from 10 below zero at Pittsburgh to 22 below zero at Accident, Maryland. Low temperatures were generally 10 to 15 degrees below zero elsewhere.

An area of low pressure passed through the Ohio Valley spreading precipitation across Maryland on the 6th and 7th. Warmer air was drawn into the storm system aloft, but surface temperatures remained below freezing resulting in freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter inch ice accumulation on all surfaces in Frostburg and Eckert Mines. Interstate 68 closed down in and near Frostburg due to accidents from icy conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the northern branch of the jet stream was able to phase with low pressure in the southern branch of the jet stream over the Southeast Coast on the night of the 18th. These two systems combined to develop a strong area of low pressure that slowly track up the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the 19th. The low was able to tap into moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean causing copious amounts of precipitation to develop. High pressure to the north kept plenty of cold air in place causing the precipitation to fall in the form of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were reported between 19 and 20 inches across the county.

The combination of the cold front and instability caused showers and thunderstorms to develop. Some thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down from thunderstorm winds in McCoole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked through the Tennessee Valley into the Ohio Valley on the 27th spreading precipitation across Maryland. Precipitation began as snow, but a change over to sleet, and eventually freezing rain occurred from south to north during the afternoon and evening of the 27th. Freezing rain finally changed to rain from south to north during the morning and afternoon hours of the 28th. Rain ended during the early evening hours of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation around one tenth of an inch was reported across the county. Snow and sleet accumulation around 2 inches was reported throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passed through the Ohio Valley spreading precipitation across Maryland on the 6th and 7th. Warmer air was drawn into the storm system aloft, but surface temperatures remained below freezing resulting in freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice due to freezing rain was reported in Millers, Manchester, and Westminster. Ice accumulation of one tenth of an inch was measured in Millers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved north across West Virginia to western Pennsylvania producing a heavy mix of precipitation. Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches across northwest Pennsylvania on the night of the 27Th before changing to freezing rain the morning of the 28Th, then back to snow in the afternoon with another 1 to 2 inches. Ice accumulations in Pittsburgh and areas north were from a quarter to one half inch. For southeast Ohio, northern West Virgina, and Garrett county Maryland mixed precipitation changed to freezing rain during the evening of the 27Th with ice quickly accumulating to one half inch before changing to plain overnight. The rain changed back to snow during the afternoon of the 28Th with 1 to 2 inches of snow accumulating.

Low pressure developed off the Mid-Atlantic coast causing precipitation to develop over Maryland. There was enough cold air in place for precipitation to start off as a wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain across locations to the north and west of Baltimore. Precipitation changed to all rain by late in the morning as warmer air was drawn into the system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation around two hundredths of an inch was estimated across the central and eastern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving slowly across West Virginia produced widespread heavy rain and flooding across northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and western Pennsylvania. Rain amounts over a 24 to 36 hour period were from 2 to 3 inches on top of soils that were already saturated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest flow between an upper-level low over the Great Lakes and high pressure over the western Atlantic ushered in plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico. The moisture combined with strong surface heating to produce an unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed due to the unstable atmosphere and some thunderstorms were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down at Route 464 and Route 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure developed off the Southeast Coast on Sunday, March 1st. This system slowly moved up the Mid-Atlantic coast Sunday night tapping into plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico as well as the Atlantic Ocean. With cold air in place, precipitation fell in the form of snow Sunday night into Monday, March 2nd. The heaviest snow fell close to the Chesapeake Bay. Snow tapered off early Monday afternoon as the storm system headed up towards New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 13.0 inches in Port Republic. Snowfall amounts averaged between 6 and 10 inches across the rest of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was reported at the junction of Maryland Route 66 and Mount Aetna Road.

This system slowly moved up the Mid-Atlantic coast Sunday night tapping into plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico as well as the Atlantic Ocean. With cold air in place, precipitation fell in the form of snow Sunday night into Monday, March 2nd. The heaviest snow fell close to the Chesapeake Bay. Snow tapered off early Monday afternoon as the storm system headed up towards New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals averaged around 2 to 5 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough and low pressure moving across the Ohio Valley pushed warm and humid air northward ahead of a cold front moving east with the surface low. Severe thunderstorms were widespread across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Significant flash flooding occurred over eastern Allegheny and western Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania with estimated damage of 18 million dollars to public infrastructure and private buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported flooding along Mill Run in Friendsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful nor'easter produced rain and gusty winds across the southern Maryland Eastern Shore from the 11th through the 14th. While rain began falling on the 11th, the heaviest rain fell from the 12th into the first half of the 13th. The rain ended on the 14th. Event precipitation totals averaged 2 to 3 inches and produced some poor drainage and low lying area flooding. Runoff from streams, creeks and rivers were slowed by the higher than normal tides.||Event precipitation totals included 3.15 inches in Trappe (Talbot County), 3.06 inches in Bellevue (Talbot County), 2.99 inches in Denton (Caroline County), 2.90 inches in Greensboro (Caroline County), 2.62 inches in Easton (Talbot County) and 1.83 inches in Saint Michaels (Talbot County).| |The nor'easter was originally Hurricane Ida, which made landfall in Alabama on the morning of the 10th. As Ida made landfall, it lost tropical characteristics and transformed into an extratropical low pressure system. On the morning of November 11th, the low pressure system reached southern Georgia and the next morning (12th) it was located over the coastal waters east of Wilmington, North Carolina. The extratropical low pressure system reached its maximum strength of around 995 millibars during the evening of the 12th as it was located just off of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina. From there, the low pressure system slowly drifted off the North Carolina coast as it slowly weakened on the 13th and 14th.

Freezing rain that started during the morning on the 13th led to several accidents across Cecil County, mainly on bridges and overpasses. Temperatures were just cold enough at the start of the precipitation for it to fall as freezing rain. Freezing rain began in parts of Cecil County between 7 a.m. EST and 8 a.m. EST. and changed over to plain rain by 10 a.m. EST. The freezing rain resulted from precipitation that formed ahead of an advancing surface warm front. While it was warm enough aloft for rain, surface temperatures lagged.
An intense nor'easter produced heavy rain across much of the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore. Rainfall amounts ranged from three to six inches over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts ranged between three and six inches across the county. Princess Anne recorded 4.69 inches of rain.

The snow ended by the middle of the evening on the 5th. The low moved rapidly northeast and at 7 p.m. EST on the 5th was already about 200 miles east of Atlantic City, New Jersey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the area on the 9th. Ahead of the front, warm and humid air led to unstable conditions. The instability combined with the boundary and an upper-level disturbance to trigger showers and thunderstorms on the afternoon and evening hours of the 9th. Some thunderstorms became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated around 50 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the northern branch of the jet stream was able to phase with low pressure in the southern branch of the jet stream over the Southeast Coast on the night of the 18th. These two systems combined to develop a strong area of low pressure that slowly track up the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the 19th. The low was able to tap into moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean causing copious amounts of precipitation to develop. High pressure to the north kept plenty of cold air in place causing the precipitation to fall in the form of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts between 17 and 19 inches were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system dropped a wintry mix of precipitation across Cecil, Kent and Queen Anne's counties in Maryland.  Snowfall averaged 1 to 2 inches with ice accretions less than one tenth of an inch. Precipitation began during the early morning on the 31st as snow in Cecil and Kent Counties and a wintry mix across Queen Anne's County. The snow changed to a wintry mix before it ended in Cecil County and plain rain before it ended elsewhere. All precipitation ended by Noon EST.  The precipitation's timing coupled with high traffic volume resulted in numerous accidents.||Specific snowfall included 1.0 inch in Millington (Kent County). The low pressure system that caused the wintry mix moved northeast from Mobile, Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the 31st and reached Wilmington, North Carolina at 1 p.m. EST and was southeast of Long Island at 7 p.m. EST that evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked through the Tennessee Valley into the Ohio Valley on the 27th spreading precipitation across Maryland. Precipitation began as snow, but a change over to sleet, and eventually freezing rain occurred from south to north during the afternoon and evening of the 27th. Freezing rain finally changed to rain from south to north during the morning and afternoon hours of the 28th. Rain ended during the early evening hours of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of one quarter inch was estimated throughout the county. |Snow and sleet accumulations were estimated around 2 to 3 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moving across the Great Lakes brought of mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain to the ridges of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Ice accumulated from 1/4 to 1/2 inch across the region the night of December 8th into the morning of December 9th before the precipitation changed to all rain.

A strong pressure gradient behind a cold front produced damaging winds across all of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, eastern Ohio, and Garrett county Maryland. Sustained winds were from 30 to 40 MPH with gusts over 60 MPH. The strongest reported wind gusts were 68 MPH in Franklin, PA and 66 MPH in Eighty Four, PA. Trees and power lines were reported down across the region, with more than 50,000 homes without power late afternoon on the 9th at the peak of the winds.

Low pressure tracked through the Tennessee Valley and emerged off the Mid-Atlantic coast on the 5th. This system spread precipitation across Maryland. There was enough cold air drawn in from the north for precipitation to fall in the form of snow across most areas. Most of the snow fell during the morning and afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Average snowfall amounts across the county were between 2 and 3 inches. However, there was a localized report of 7.0 inches of snow in Norrisville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A upper level storm system over the Midwest United States allowed for abundant moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean to override cold air at the surface across the mid-Atlantic. A surface low pressure developed off the coast of North Carolina and moved northward up the East Coast. With anomalously high precipitable water content across the region, widespread heavy rainfall with with amounts of 1 to 2 inches prompted areas of flooding St Mary's County in Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Creek out of its banks, flooding nearby parking along MD-243 near MD-5. Spotter in region observed 2.48 inches for storm total rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense Nor'easter produced moderate to severe coastal flooding across portions of the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets, homes and businesses were flooded in low lying areas of the county close or directly exposed to the Chesapeake Bay. The dual highway leading to the lower downtown section of Crisfield was closed to traffic for a time period. Smith Island had tide water that covered Ewell, Rhodes Point, and Tylerton. Ewell Church and the Fire Department had 3 to 4 inches of water in buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense Nor'easter produced moderate to severe coastal flooding across portions of the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak tide height at Ocean City was 5.44 feet above MLLW, which was 3.17 feet above the astronomical tide. Numerous streets, homes and businesses were flooded in low lying areas of the county close or directly exposed to the Atlantic Ocean. Ocean City reported a major loss of the beach. The Corps of Engineers and the Ocean City Engineer estimated 1 million cubic yards of sand were lost, and approximately 50 percent of the Dune Line was lost. In addition, there was considerable damage to the beaches on Assateague National Seashore. More than 1500 tires from an offshore artificial reef washed up on to Assateague's beaches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system over the midwestern United States brought warm southerly winds to the area, helping to melt a deep snowpack over the Mid-Atlantic. An area of low pressure moved northward up the east coast of the United States. This system brough between 1.5 and 2 inches of rain to much of Maryland on December 25 into December 26th. Combined with snowmelt, the rain caused flooding across portions of Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water street in Marlow Heights closed due to flooding. Observer near Marlow Heights reported 1.67 rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense Nor'easter produced moderate to severe coastal flooding across portions of the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak tide height at Cambridge was 3.69 feet above MLLW, which was 1.67 feet above the astronomical tide. Several streets, homes and businesses were flooded in low lying areas of the county close or directly exposed to the Chesapeake Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense Nor'easter produced moderate to severe coastal flooding across portions of the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets, homes and businesses were flooded in low lying areas of the county close or directly exposed to the Atlantic Ocean and Chincoteague Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southwest winds occurred during the day of the 9th preceding a cold frontal passage late in the afternoon. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and coupled with the heavy rain, helped knock down shallow rooted trees, weak tree limbs and wires across the Eastern Shore. In Federalsburg (Caroline County), about 450 people lost power after a downed tree took down a transmission pole. On December 10th and 11th, gusty west winds prevailed, but peak wind gusts were on average a few mph lower than on the 9th. Peak wind gusts on the 9th included 45 mph at Thomas Point (Kent County) and 41 mph in Salisbury (Wicomico County).

A major winter storm affected the Eastern Shore on the 19th and 20th. Heavy snow fell across Cecil, Kent and Queen Anne's Counties and heavy snow and sleet fell across Caroline and Talbot Counties. Snowfall averaged 15 to 22 inches. Snow began around Midnight EST on the 19th and fell at its heaviest during the early morning along the southern Eastern Shore and during the afternoon along the northern Eastern Shore. Sleet mixed in with the snow and for a while was the only precipitation type between 8 a.m. EST and Noon EST on the 19th in Caroline and Talbot Counties before it changed back to snow again.  The snow ended between 1 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST on the 20th from south to north.||The winter storm postponed many planned local events. It came at an inopportune time for retailers as the Saturday (the 19th) before Christmas is usually one of the busiest shopping days of the year. Many municipalities declared states of emergency. Many school districts either closed schools or had two hour delayed openings on the 21st. Some churches cancelled services on the 20th. Trash pick-ups were delayed. ||Representative snowfall included 22.0 inches in Millington (Kent County), 17.0 inches in Elkton (Cecil County) and Denton (Caroline County), 13.0 inches in Centreville (Queen Anne's County) and 12.0 inches in Easton (Talbot County). ||The nor'easter responsible for the winter storm formed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 17th. It moved northeast along the northern Gulf of Mexico and was just south of Mobile, Alabama at sunrise on the 18th. From there it moved northeast and continued to intensify and reached Savannah, Georgia around 7 p.m. EST on the 18th, near Cape Hatteras, North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 19th. It continued to move northeast on the 19th and into the 20th and by sunrise on the 20th was located about 400 miles east of Seaside Heights, New Jersey.

The combination of melting snow and heavy rain produced field and  poor drainage flooding along the Eastern Shore, especially in Cecil County. Rain began falling during the evening of the 25th and fell at a nearly state rate, until it ended about twenty-four hours later during the evening of the 26th. Event precipitation totals average 1 to 2 inches with the highest amounts in Cecil County. ||The steady rain was caused by a strong southerly flow of air from the Gulf of Mexico around an intense low pressure system that spent much of the 25th and 26th in Iowa before weakening and drifting to the northeast. The rain ended after this low's occluded front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Event precipitation totals included 2.06 inches in Elkton and 2.02 inches in Colora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes on the 9th produced a strong southerly flow of moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean. Rain began falling during the evening of the 8th and ended by Noon EST on the 9th. It fell heavy at times during the early morning of the 9th. Event precipitation totals averaged two to three inches across the Eastern Shore. The concentration of the heavy rain in a relatively short period of time produced poor drainage flooding and field flooding. It also caused a couple of waterways in Caroline County to flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Event precipitation totals included 1.88 inches in Stevensville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts between 14 and 25 inches were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through Maryland early in the day. Behind this front, an area of snow developed and moved across the area late in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of 2.0 snow accumulation was received from Smithsburg. Various lesser amounts were received from across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through Maryland early in the day. Behind this front, an area of snow developed and moved across the area late in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of 2.5 of snow was received from Smithburg. Several lesser amounts were reported across eastern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the Eastern Shore on the 19th and 20th. Heavy snow fell across Cecil, Kent and Queen Anne's Counties and heavy snow and sleet fell across Caroline and Talbot Counties. Snowfall averaged 15 to 22 inches. Snow began around Midnight EST on the 19th and fell at its heaviest during the early morning along the southern Eastern Shore and during the afternoon along the northern Eastern Shore. Sleet mixed in with the snow and for a while was the only precipitation type between 8 a.m. EST and Noon EST on the 19th in Caroline and Talbot Counties before it changed back to snow again.  The snow ended between 1 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST on the 20th from south to north.||The winter storm postponed many planned local events. It came at an inopportune time for retailers as the Saturday (the 19th) before Christmas is usually one of the busiest shopping days of the year. Many municipalities declared states of emergency. Many school districts either closed schools or had two hour delayed openings on the 21st. Some churches cancelled services on the 20th. Trash pick-ups were delayed. ||Representative snowfall included 22.0 inches in Millington (Kent County), 17.0 inches in Elkton (Cecil County) and Denton (Caroline County), 13.0 inches in Centreville (Queen Anne's County) and 12.0 inches in Easton (Talbot County). ||The nor'easter responsible for the winter storm formed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 17th. It moved northeast along the northern Gulf of Mexico and was just south of Mobile, Alabama at sunrise on the 18th. From there it moved northeast and continued to intensify and reached Savannah, Georgia around 7 p.m. EST on the 18th, near Cape Hatteras, North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 19th. It continued to move northeast on the 19th and into the 20th and by sunrise on the 20th was located about 400 miles east of Seaside Heights, New Jersey.

A frontal boundary was draped from the southern Mid Atlantic region southwestward to the northern Gulf of Mexico. An area of low pressure developed along this front across the lower Mississippi Valley late in the day on the 27th. The storm system then moved northeastward and tracked near the Ohio River Valley during the morning of the 28th. As the storm continued to track northeastward, it strengthened as it moved into New England during the early evening hours of the 28th. An associated cold front then swept across the Mid Atlantic region during the early evening hours of the 28th.||A cold air mass ahead of the system allowed for snow to overspread the area generally from south to north during the morning of the 27th. As milder air then worked in especially above the surface, the snow mixed with sleet and pockets of freezing rain at times during the night of the 27th. The temperatures slowly climbed to freezing toward daybreak on the 28th, then the precipitation began to taper off during the morning of the 28th. The combination of the frozen and freezing precipitation along with initially cold air, created a high impact event and produced very slippery travel.||Some snowfall totals included; 2.5 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County, 1.8 inches 2.4 miles southeast of Easton (Talbot County), 1.7 inches 1.4 miles east-northeast of Greensboro (Cecil County), 1.7 inches 0.7 of a mile southeast of St. Michaels (Talbot County) and 1.0 inch 0.3 of a mile north-northeast of Bellevue (Talbot County). Ice accumulations in Cecil, Kent and Queen Anne's counties were around one-quarter of an inch. Due to a quicker changeover to plain rain, there was less icing in Caroline and Talbot counties.

A large arctic high pressure system moved toward the area during the 16th and 17th. The extent of the arctic air mass kept maximum temperatures only in the teens and 20s, with minimum temperatures down into the single digits. As a storm system remained well to the northeast, the combination of this along with strong arctic high pressure to the west allowed for gusty winds. This produced wind chill values around zero for awhile, especially during the nighttime hours.||Temperatures in the single digits during the early morning of the 17th caused water pipes to break throughout the eastern shore region. Broken water pipes were reported at the Bethlehem Mart in Bethlehem, The Bridge Restaurant on Tilghman Island and at two churches; the church of God in Gransonville and at Saints Peter and Paul Catholic Church in Easton. The weekend masses were cancelled at Saints Peter and Paul due to a sprinkler system pipe that broke and flooded the church sanctuary.||The temperature in Easton dropped to 12 degrees on the morning of the 16th, then bottomed out at 7 on the morning of the 17th.

An area of low pressure developed across the Gulf Coasts states during the 27th. A warm front extended northeastward from this storm into the southern Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a cold dome of air was entrenched over the area while milder air gradually moved northward in association with the warm front. As the milder and even more moist air arrived over the snow on the ground, areas of dense fog developed across the eastern portions of the state on the 28th. The local visibilities lowered to under one-quarter of a mile at times during the height of the dense fog. A cold front moved through during the early evening of the 28th, which quickly dissipated the areas of dense fog.||The Easton Airport (Talbot County) reported a visibility of one-eighth of a mile between 12 PM EST and 5 PM EST on the 28th.

A powerful cold front moved across the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 11th as strong low pressure moved north across the Great Lakes. A convective line along the cold front produced widespread damage across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland with thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH and a peak wind gust of 92 MPH at Allegheny County Airport near Pittsburgh. High winds gusting over 60 MPH behind the front in a strong pressure gradient produced wind damage across the entire region into midday on the 12th. In all across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland over one half million homes and businesses were without power at some point during the storm.

Cold northwest flow moving across the Great Lakes brought heavy bands of snow to the higher elevations of Northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland. Total snowfall was from 8 to 12 inches across the region.

Strong winds occurred just after Midnight EST on March 2nd along most of the Eastern Shore as a gravity wave helped mix stronger winds aloft to the ground. The combination of the wind and heavy snow helped cause power outages across the Delmarva Peninsula. Delmarva Power and Light reported about 26,000 homes and businesses lost power in its service area. About 16,000 of the outages were in Kent, Queen Anne's, Caroline and Talbot Counties. All power was restored by Noon EST on the 3rd.  Choptank Electric reported about 8,700 homes and businesses lost power along the Eastern Shore with the most outages in Kent and Cecil Counties. Most power was restored by the afternoon of the 2nd. The strong winds also caused considerable blowing and drifting of snow and made it difficult for crews to keep roads plowed and open. The strong winds were associated with the nor'easter that brought the heavy snow to the Eastern Shore. The nor'easter low pressure system moved from the Georgia and South Carolina border at 7 a.m. EST on the 1st to about 150 miles east of the southern New Jersey coast at 7 a.m. EST on the 2nd. The strongest winds occurred during the early morning of the 2nd as a gravity wave that emanated from the low pressure system passed across the state.

A cold front which followed an unseasonably warm and humid late March afternoon helped trigger a severe thunderstorm in Cecil County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in North East. One large pine tree fell onto and caused minor damage to one home.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS site at Oakland, Maryland measured a wind gust to 51 KTs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to high winds affected the Eastern Shore during the day on the 12th. The strong winds started shortly after a cold frontal passage between 3 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST and persisted through most of the day. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and knocked down several tree limbs, weak trees and power lines. Delmarva Power and Light reported about 4,600 homes and businesses in Delaware and Cecil County lost power. The Cecil County outages were centered around North East and Charlestown. Several roads were closed in Cecil County because of downed trees in North East, Elkton and U.S. Route 222 between Port Deposit and the Conowingo Dam. A downed tree damaged a vehicle in Elkton. In Caroline County, in Preston, the single support post of a roof covering at a Valero gas station snapped at 2 p.m. EST. No vehicles or pumps were damaged. In Queen Anne's County the strong west winds sent waves crashing into the bulkheads at Matapeake Park on Kent Island. Flying spray was blown well inland.  ||Peak wind gusts included 54 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport, 50 mph in Tolchester Beach (Kent County), 48 mph in Salisbury (Wicomico County), 47 mph in Ridgely (Caroline County) and 43 mph in Chesapeake City (Cecil County).||The strong and high winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that moved through the lower Saint Lawrence River Valley, northern New England and the Canadian Maritimes and an approaching high pressure system from the lower Mississippi Valley. The winds increased after a cold front moved through the region between 3 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the morning of the 12th and slowly subsided that night.

Strong gusty west winds followed a cold front passage across the Maryland Eastern Shore during the afternoon and evening of the 19th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 45 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport and 40 mph in Salisbury (Wicomico County). The gusty winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that moved northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system that approached from the Mississippi Valley.

An area of low pressure rapidly intensified over southern Canada. The pressure gradient between this strong low and high pressure to our south resulted in gusty winds across western Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 50 knots was reported at Frostburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure developed off the Southeast Coast on Sunday, March 1st. This system slowly moved up the Mid-Atlantic coast Sunday night tapping into plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico as well as the Atlantic Ocean. With cold air in place, precipitation fell in the form of snow Sunday night into Monday, March 2nd. The heaviest snow fell close to the Chesapeake Bay. Snow tapered off early Monday afternoon as the storm system headed up towards New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 11.0 inches in Hollywood and Leonardtown. Ridge reported 9.2 inches of snow. There were also reports of trees and powerlines down due to the heavy snow.

Strong to high winds affected the Eastern Shore during the day on the 12th. The strong winds started shortly after a cold frontal passage between 3 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST and persisted through most of the day. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and knocked down several tree limbs, weak trees and power lines. Delmarva Power and Light reported about 4,600 homes and businesses in Delaware and Cecil County lost power. The Cecil County outages were centered around North East and Charlestown. Several roads were closed in Cecil County because of downed trees in North East, Elkton and U.S. Route 222 between Port Deposit and the Conowingo Dam. A downed tree damaged a vehicle in Elkton. In Caroline County, in Preston, the single support post of a roof covering at a Valero gas station snapped at 2 p.m. EST. No vehicles or pumps were damaged. In Queen Anne's County the strong west winds sent waves crashing into the bulkheads at Matapeake Park on Kent Island. Flying spray was blown well inland.  ||Peak wind gusts included 54 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport, 50 mph in Tolchester Beach (Kent County), 48 mph in Salisbury (Wicomico County), 47 mph in Ridgely (Caroline County) and 43 mph in Chesapeake City (Cecil County).||The strong and high winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that moved through the lower Saint Lawrence River Valley, northern New England and the Canadian Maritimes and an approaching high pressure system from the lower Mississippi Valley. The winds increased after a cold front moved through the region between 3 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the morning of the 12th and slowly subsided that night.

Strong gusty west winds followed a cold front passage across the Maryland Eastern Shore during the afternoon and evening of the 19th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 45 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport and 40 mph in Salisbury (Wicomico County). The gusty winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that moved northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system that approached from the Mississippi Valley.

A winter storm occurred across the Eastern Shore from the late afternoon of the 1st into the late afternoon of the 2nd. About eight to twelve inches of snow fell across the region and caused a death in Queen Anne's County. A snow emergency was in effect for the state from the 1st into the 4th. The combination of strong winds and heavy snow made travel difficult at best and made it tough for road crews to keep thoroughfares opened. There were many fender benders and vehicles that slid off roads. Numerous accidents were reported. The Mid-Atlantic AAA responded to 2,600 calls for assistance throughout their region. All schools as well as many county and municipal offices and businesses were closed on Monday March 2nd. many meetings were postponed. Schools did not reopen until the 3rd in Cecil County, 4th in Talbot and Queen Anne's County and the 5th in Kent and Caroline Counties. ||The one weather related death occurred in Queen Anne's County. A 79-year-old man was found dead from hypothermia at about 730 p.m. EST on the 2nd on Dill Farm Road off of White Marsh Road. His wife was found inside the Sports Utility Vehicle that became stuck in a ditch for a second time. The vehicle apparently became stuck on Clannihan Shop Road at about 230 a.m. EST on the 2nd near Lieby Road. A snow plow driver pulled the vehicle out of the ditch. Shortly after 7 a.m. EST, the vehicle was spotted in about the same area. The husband apparently left the vehicle looking for help. It was assumed that the heavy falling and drifting snow disoriented him. His body was found across the fields from Clannihan Shop Road. The wife was treated for hypothermia.   ||Precipitation began as a mixture of rain and sleet across the lower Eastern Shore and just plain snow elsewhere during the late afternoon of the 1st. The mixed precipitation changed to all snow by the start of the evening of the 1st and fell heavy at times during the evening and overnight as gravity wave bands of heavier snow moved through the region. Snow of generally lighter intensity fell during the day on the 2nd, before it ended during the late afternoon.||In Cecil County, a tanker truck filled with liquid propane overturned on a snow covered road near Elkton at 10 a.m. EST on the 2nd. This caused the evacuation of about 15 homes. They returned by 6 p.m. EST that evening. In Queen Anne's County, in addition to the weather related death, a 47-year-old woman was injured after she slid into another vehicle on Maryland State Route 301. Wind restrictions were in effect on the William Preston Lane Jr. Memorial Bridge across Chesapeake Bay until the afternoon of the 2nd. In Caroline County, 28 accidents were reported. In Talbot County, Easton Airport was closed from 10 p.m. EST on the 1st through 2 p.m. EST on the 2nd. State Police responded to 41 weather related accidents. Fifty vehicles became stuck in ditches along a thirty-one mile stretch of U.S. Route 50 between Easton (Talbot County) and Vienna (Dorchester County). The cold weather that followed caused a pipe to burst in a school in Trappe (Talbot County).||Snowfall totals included 12.2 inches in Royal Oak (Talbot County), 11.0 inches in Chestertown and Millington (both Kent County), 10.3 inches in Centreville (Queen Anne's County), 9.0 inches in Galena (Kent County), 8.8 inches in Ridgely (Caroline County), 8.5 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County), 8.0 inches in Elkton (Cecil County), 7.5 inches in Easton (Talbot County), 5.5 inches in Denton (Caroline County) and Saint Michaels (Talbot County) and 5.0 inches in Port Deposit (Cecil County). For some places this was the heaviest single snow event since February of 2003. ||The winter storm was caused by a nor'easter that moved east of the region. The nor'easter low pressure system moved from the Georgia and South Carolina border at 7 a.m. EST on the 1st to about 150 miles east of the southern New Jersey coast at 7 a.m. EST on the 2nd. It was passing through the Canadian Maritimes during the morning of the 3rd. Heavy snow bands associated with the gravity wave occurred during the pre-dawn hours on the 2nd when most of the heaviest snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred just after Midnight EST on March 2nd along most of the Eastern Shore as a gravity wave helped mix stronger winds aloft to the ground. The combination of the wind and heavy snow helped cause power outages across the Delmarva Peninsula. Delmarva Power and Light reported about 26,000 homes and businesses lost power in its service area. About 16,000 of the outages were in Kent, Queen Anne's, Caroline and Talbot Counties. All power was restored by Noon EST on the 3rd.  Choptank Electric reported about 8,700 homes and businesses lost power along the Eastern Shore with the most outages in Kent and Cecil Counties. Most power was restored by the afternoon of the 2nd. The strong winds also caused considerable blowing and drifting of snow and made it difficult for crews to keep roads plowed and open. The strong winds were associated with the nor'easter that brought the heavy snow to the Eastern Shore. The nor'easter low pressure system moved from the Georgia and South Carolina border at 7 a.m. EST on the 1st to about 150 miles east of the southern New Jersey coast at 7 a.m. EST on the 2nd. The strongest winds occurred during the early morning of the 2nd as a gravity wave that emanated from the low pressure system passed across the state.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal low pressure produced between one half inch and three inches of snow across portions of the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore from late Sunday afternoon, March 1st, into Monday morning March 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between one half inch and three inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure moved out of the Ohio Valley, across the Appalachians, and then through the mid-Atlantic during the day. The low produced widespread rain and thunderstorms, some of which caused flooding problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches caused streams and tributaries to flow out of their banks. In Bowling Green, 12 basements were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure riding along a stalled boundary triggered rain and thunderstorms. Heavy amounts of rain in a short period of time lead to isolated areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sligo Creek Parkway was closed between Piney Branch Road and Wayne Avenue due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to locally severe thunderstorms preceded a cold front and moved through Maryland during the afternoon of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires in central Kent County. About 400 homes and businesses lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure riding along a stalled boundary triggered rain and thunderstorms. Heavy amounts of rain in a short period of time lead to isolated areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sunnyside Avenue at Edmonston Road was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure riding along a stalled boundary triggered rain and thunderstorms. Heavy amounts of rain in a short period of time lead to isolated areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported just east of Laurel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very slow moving and intense low pressure system was responsible for strong northwest winds across the Eastern Shore from the evening of the 3rd through the afternoon of the 4th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph. Some higher wind gusts occurred on the western side of Chesapeake Bay.  Peak wind gusts included 58 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport and 43 mph in Annapolis and Salisbury (Wicomico County). ||The strong northwest winds began after a cold front moved through the Eastern Shore during the early evening on the 3rd. It persisted until the low pressure system associated with that front moved farther northeast up the Saint Lawrence River Valley and started to weaken.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm turned over a carport onto a swing set off of Dover Road in Easton. A large tree was also knocked down between two houses on Prospect Avenue. A mesonet site at Cook's Hope measured a wind gust to 50 mph.

A boundary remained stationary over the region. This boundary combined with unstable air to trigger showers and thunderstorms across Maryland. Some thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening hours were severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was reported in Friendship.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to locally severe thunderstorms moved across the Maryland Eastern Shore during the afternoon of the 29th. The thunderstorms preceded a cold front that moved through the Middle Atlantic States during the night of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a line of trees along Eastern Neck Road south of Rock Hall. A travel trailer was destroyed by a downed tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to locally severe thunderstorms moved across the Maryland Eastern Shore during the afternoon of the 29th. The thunderstorms preceded a cold front that moved through the Middle Atlantic States during the night of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in Centreville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow southward moving cold front located over the Philadelphia Delaware Valley during the morning of June 3rd helped trigger severe thunderstorms in the Maryland Eastern Shore during that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell south of Galena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the Maryland Eastern Shore during the early evening of June 11th. These thunderstorms formed close to a nearly stationary frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires on the southern part of Kent Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of a cold front produced large hail and damaging winds across portions of the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms formed near a warm frontal boundary and moved across the Eastern Shore during the late afternoon on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms tore down tree limbs and power lines mainly in central Queen Anne's County. The combination of the wind and lightning caused about 1,200 Delmarva Power and Light customers to lose power in the county, which was the hardest hit along the Maryland Eastern Shore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure riding along a stalled boundary triggered rain and thunderstorms. Heavy amounts of rain in a short period of time lead to isolated areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Muirkirk Road was closed near Route 1 due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong thunderstorms moved through the Maryland Eastern Shore during the morning of June 9th. Lightning from the thunderstorms caused outages in Talbot County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike that hit a power line caused outages to about 300 customers in the Easton Utility service area in Easton. Traffic lights were also knocked out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance coupled with a weak surface trof helped develop widely scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across the Maryland Eastern Shore during the afternoon and early evening of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted near the Bethel Wesleyan Church in Goldsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system over the Great Lakes allowed a surface front to retreat northward through the mid-Atlantic, with thunderstorms developing along the feature across northern Virgina and southern Maryland during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported in Hughesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure rapidly intensified over southern Canada. The pressure gradient between this strong low and high pressure to our south resulted in gusty winds across Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 50 knots was reported at KBWI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow southward moving cold front located over the Leatherstocking Region of New York State during the morning of June 2nd helped trigger thunderstorms along the eastern shore during that afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Bay View couple was homeless after a lightning strike and ensuing fire badly damaged their home. Lightning struck a tree on the property and then jumped to the roof of the home. It took about sixty firefighters ninety minutes to extinguish the blaze. Motorists were detoured near Cecil College as the fire was being fought. No injuries were reported. Damage was estimated at $215,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that formed along a warm front caused lightning damage in Caroline County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and the ensuing fire destroyed an abandoned barn outside of Henderson along Bee Tree Road in Caroline County. A nearby tree was initially struck by the lightning. About twenty-seven fire fighters from four separate companies responded and took about thirty minutes to control the blaze. But, the two story barn was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger a line of thunderstorms during the evening of the 21st in Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused 31 homes to lose power in Preston. Power was restored to all homes by 11 p.m. EDT that evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary remained stationary over the region. This boundary combined with unstable air to trigger showers and thunderstorms across Maryland. Some thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening hours were severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on Poorhouse Road and Route 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary remained stationary over the region. This boundary combined with unstable air to trigger showers and thunderstorms across Maryland. Some thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening hours were severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was reported just east-southeast of Rockville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorm in advance of a frontal boundary produced damaging winds across portions of Wicomico county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were downed in Salisbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the Eastern Shore of Maryland during the evening of the 25th near a low pressure system that formed along the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees, wires and telephone poles in Easton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the Eastern Shore of Maryland during the evening of the 25th near a low pressure system that formed along the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped penny to nickel size hail in Centreville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the Eastern Shore of Maryland during the evening of the 25th near a low pressure system that formed along the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees in Preston. A downed tree also tore down a transformer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid air mass combined with an approaching surface trof to produce strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening across the Maryland Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell from a severe thunderstorm in Easton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance helped trigger an isolated severe thunderstorm in the northern part of the Eastern Shore during the early evening of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell from a severe thunderstorm in Colora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid air mass combined with an approaching surface trof to produce strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening across the Maryland Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Port Deposit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong size hail was reported near Walnut Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with an unstable atmosphere to trigger showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of the third. An isolated thunderstorm did become severe, producing large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near Mechanicsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with an unstable atmosphere to trigger showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of the third. An isolated thunderstorm did become severe, producing large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near Mechanicsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level trough of low pressure triggered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of the 18th. An isolated thunderstorm did become severe, producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were down in La Plata.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere lead to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across Maryland. Some thunderstorms did become severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree two diameter in feet fell across Highway 257.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere lead to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across Maryland. Some thunderstorms did become severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down near Cobb Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere lead to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across Maryland. Some thunderstorms did become severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were snapped. A tree also fell onto a house near Swan Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere lead to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across Maryland. Some thunderstorms did become severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail was reported near Middle River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere lead to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across Maryland. Some thunderstorms did become severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service survey indicated an EF-0 tornado touched down near Pleasant Hills before dissipating near the Atkisson Resorvoir. Peak wind speeds were estimated around 75 mph.  ||Several trees were down in Pleasant Hills and also near the intersection of Hollingsworth Drive and Ring Factory Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pressure trough combined with warm and humid conditions to trigger showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of June 2nd. The warm and humid conditions caused plenty of instability and this lead to some of the thunderstorms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 64 mph was measured at Patuxent River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere lead to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across Maryland. Some thunderstorms did become severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service survey indicated that a tornado touched down near Essex as an EF-0. Peak winds were estimated around 70 mph. Several trees were down and there was minor shingle and siding damage reported throughout the community of Essex. ||The tornado intensified as it approached Browns Creek and Balliston Point. The tornado uprooted or snapped down a significant portion of large trees in the community along Island View Road. The tornado across this area was classified as an EF-1 with peak winds of 90 mph. The tornado finally weakened as it moved over the Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near the Hagerstown Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported about three miles east of Andrews Air Force Base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure moving along a slow moving cold front produced scattered severe thunderstorms across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland.

An isolated severe thunderstorm affected southern parts of the Maryland Eastern Shore during the late afternoon on the 13th. The thunderstorms formed near an inverted trof boundary that extended north from a low pressure system in North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn spotter estimated wind gusts of 60 mph in a severe thunderstorm near Denton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds gust associated with a shower caused a pilot to lose control on a runway on the Stevensville Bay Bridge Airport on the afternoon of the 15th. The Beechcraft Airplane touched down on the runway when it was hit by a wind gust. The pilot lost control and the plane flipped nose down. Three fire companies responded to the ensuing fuel leak. There was no fire or injuries. The wind gust was estimated at 30 knots.

An upper-level disturbance combined with plenty of moisture and an unstable atmosphere to trigger showers and thunderstorms during on the 9th. With locally heavy rain in a short period of time, this lead to isolated areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beach Drive was closed between the Kensington Parkway and Beltway due to a water rescue from flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to locally severe thunderstorms occurred in the Maryland Eastern Shore during the afternoon of the 20th. The area barely made it into the warm sector (south of the warm front and east of the approaching cold front) of a low pressure system that moved through Pennsylvania and New Jersey on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust front from a severe thunderstorm flattened a corn field (was year old corn) off of Auckland Road near Love Point on Kent Island. This was the same parent thunderstorm that produced waterspouts on Chesapeake Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to locally severe thunderstorms occurred in the Maryland Eastern Shore during the afternoon of the 20th. The area barely made it into the warm sector (south of the warm front and east of the approaching cold front) of a low pressure system that moved through Pennsylvania and New Jersey on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and wires throughout Cecil County. The combination of damaging winds and lightning caused about 2,000 homes and businesses in the county to lose power. Power was not fully restored until the afternoon of the 21st. Heavy rain from the thunderstorm also flooded Delaware Avenue in Elkton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. With plenty of instability and strong winds aloft, thunderstorms did become severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large branches were broken by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. With plenty of instability and strong winds aloft, thunderstorms did become severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several mid-size branches were down from thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. With plenty of instability and strong winds aloft, thunderstorms did become severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down near Route 70 and Andover Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn spotter saw a funnel cloud northwest of Denton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires between Agner and Federalsburg in Caroline County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down on homes in Herald Harbor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down in Belvedere Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. With plenty of instability and strong winds aloft, thunderstorms did become severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down near Forked Creek Road and Shore Acres.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near Severna Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. With plenty of instability and strong winds aloft, thunderstorms did become severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported near Arnold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. With plenty of instability and strong winds aloft, thunderstorms did become severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported near Ripley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. With plenty of instability and strong winds aloft, thunderstorms did become severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported near Port Tobacco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front arriving from the west late in the day on the 2nd moved into a warm and moist air mass, which resulted in thunderstorms across the area with locally torrential rainfall during the late morning and early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flooding of smaller streams and roadways in western Cecil County. Underpasses in Perryville were flooded. North Main Street in Port Deposit was impassable. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around 3 inches in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front arriving from the west late in the day on the 2nd moved into a warm and moist air mass, which resulted in thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused wind damage in the Forest View Trailer Park and Sherwood Forest Trailer Park near Elkton. One mobile home was condemned after a tree fell through it. Another mobile home lost about 75 percent of its shingles. There were also a large number of downed trees around the area of Steel's Motel on U.S. Route 40. About 10,000 homes and businesses lost power in Cecil and Hartford Counties. Power was restored by 11 p.m. EDT on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with plenty of instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. With plenty of instability and strong winds aloft, thunderstorms did become severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported just southeast of Faulkner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary settled southward across most of the area during the 28th before stalling nearby. Plenty of moisture flowing northward along with incoming energy resulted in some showers and thunderstorms, some of which produced heavy rainfall and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flooding around White Swan Lake east of Cayots in Cecil County. Old Telegraph Road was closed. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure over the region caused showers and thunderstorms to develop. There was enough cold air aloft for some thunderstorms to become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Sewell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal low pressure system slowly moved up the Delmarva Peninsula through the day, bringing a prolonged period of light to moderate rain to northeastern Maryland, with several rounds of heavy rain during a 24 hour period. This led to flooding across Harford and Baltimore Counties. Widespread rainfall of 3 to 5 inches was observed over these areas, with the highest total reported near Jacksonville, where 5.26 inches was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Edwards Lane closed due to flooding from Shucks Road to Asbury Road. Emergency Manager reported rain gauge measurement of 3.96 inches northwest of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved into the area late on the 21st, which ran into a very warm and humid air mass. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front from west to east, however some storms were backbuilding over the same areas. Locally torrential rainfall occurred, which resulted in areas of flash flooding especially during the night time hours. For exampled, a measurement from Ridgely came in with a storm total of 9.55 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential downpours from nearly stationary thunderstorms caused major damage to several roads and properties in Ridgely, Greensboro and Goldsboro in Caroline County. A rainfall measurement from Ridgely came in with a storm total of 9.55 inches of rain through 7 a.m. EDT on the 22nd. In Ridgely, seven roads including Maryland State Road 480 were closed due to flooding and three (Central Avenue, Holly Road and Peaviner Road) of them are expected to be closed for a while due to roadway damage. A restaurant on State Route 480 was damaged. A Maryland Avenue home suffered a partial wall collapse and major basement flooding. At least one water rescue was performed. Muscachie Farm was flooded. In Greensboro, the Choptank River flooded and closed Choptank River Road. Two roads were closed in Goldsboro and Maryland State Route 311 was damaged in Henderson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front was approaching from the west during the 22nd, while Hurricane Bill passed well to our east. An abundance of moisture in place combined with enough instability, which resulted in showers and thunderstorms with locally torrential rainfall and flooding. Two day event totals averaged 3 to 6 inches, but amounts exceeded one foot locally in Ridgely in Caroline County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours rapidly caused flash flooding of smaller streams and roadways in central Caroline County. Up to nearly ten inches of rain fell that morning and around an additional two to three inches of rain fell that afternoon and evening. Event precipitation totals included 13.13 inches in Ridgely and 6.65 inches in Denton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving warm front during the morning of the 9th, produced some showers and thunderstorms. As the region became entrenched in the warm sector during the day, this helped to ignite some additional thunderstorms through the evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in and around Galena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving warm front during the morning of the 9th, produced some showers and thunderstorms. As the region became entrenched in the warm sector during the day, this helped to ignite some additional thunderstorms through the evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line of severe thunderstorms caused wind damage across the northern tier of Cecil County. Numerous trees were knocked down. The hardest hit area was in the northeast part of the county around Fair Hill. Trees fell into pools, driveways and at least one home in the Thabar Development off of Little Egypt Road and on Black Oak Drive. Patio furniture was also tossed. In North East, a tree fell onto a trailer. About 12,000 homes and businesses lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of high pressure was located off the East Coast on the 10th, a hot and very humid air mass (dew points in the mid to even upper 70s) was across the area. The heat index at Easton peaked at 105 degrees as the dew point was at 77 degrees. High temperatures were mainly in the mid 90s. A cold front moved through that night and ended the brief spell of hot weather.

A frontal boundary in the area along with a weak area of low pressure combined to produce areas of showers and thunderstorms on the 12th, mainly in the late afternoon and early evening hours. The showers and thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall, which resulted in areas of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused poor drainage flooding and flooding of smaller streams in the southeastern part of Cecil County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3 and 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of high pressure on the 18th was off the Mid Atlantic coast, while a cold front was well off to the west. In between these two systems was a surface trough, which helped to focus showers and thunderstorms within a warm and humid air mass. The thunderstorms, some severe, marched across the region during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in and around Fair Hill in northeast Cecil County. Many trees were knocked down on Maryland State Route 213. One downed tree damaged a deck. About 8,400 homes and businesses lost power in Cecil County. All power was restored by the morning of the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass was in place on the 21st ahead of a cold front. This resulted in the development of some thunderstorms from west to east, some of which were strong to locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms knocked down about 5 trees in Rock Hall and Fairee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass was in place on the 21st ahead of a cold front. This resulted in the development of some thunderstorms from west to east, some of which were strong to locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms knocked down a few trees along U.S. Route 301.

The heat index at Easton peaked at 105 degrees as the dew point was at 77 degrees. High temperatures were mainly in the mid 90s. A cold front moved through that night and ended the brief spell of hot weather.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooded forced the closure of Patterson Mill Road between Woodring Drive and Wheel Road.  A trained spotter measured a rainfall amount of 4.15 inches in a 24 hour period.

This led to flooding across Harford and Baltimore Counties. Widespread rainfall of 3 to 5 inches was observed over these areas, with the highest total reported near Jacksonville, where 5.26 inches was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at Ruxton Road and Circle Road. Numerous rainfall reports north and east of the area were received of 4 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layer easterly flow allowed a surge of Atlantic moisture to spread westward across the Chesapeake Bay. Scattered slowly moving showers developed as this area of enhanced moisture moved over lower southern Maryland, creating an isolated flash flood event in Anne Arundel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sunrise Beach Road between General's Highway and Old Herald Harbor Road was closed due to flooding. COOP Observer near the area reported 2.75 inches of rain around an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front arriving from the west late in the day on the 2nd moved into a warm and moist air mass, which resulted in thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted over Perryville. It never touched down, but the parent thunderstorm produced wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front arriving from the west late in the day on the 2nd moved into a warm and moist air mass, which resulted in thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a few trees in Federalsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the Mid-Atlantic States sandwiched between a cold front approaching from the west and Hurricane Bill several hundred miles offshore, an extremely moist atmosphere in place. A mesoscale area of low pressure formed over central Virginia and worked east-northeastward toward lower southern Maryland during the late afternoon and evening of the 22nd, produce a wide area of rain and thunderstorms that produced heavy rain and flash flooding. Numerous rainfall reports of 3 to 5 inches were received, with one report of 6.35 inches in North Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads between 3rd Street and 6th Street were closed in North Beach. Spotter reported 6.35 inches of rain in North Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and its associated trough of low pressure triggered showers and thunderstorms across Maryland. A southwest flow ahead of the front ushered in plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico, resulting in an unstable atmosphere. The moderate amounts of instability combined with the forcing from the front and strong winds aloft for some thunderstorms to become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sporadic tree damage was noted along Tabler Road, where large branches were broken from hardwood trees. The circulation impacted several residences along Ball Road between Tabler Road and Ijamsville Road. One residence suffered roof structure damage and broken windows. In this location the roof was uplifted and shifted northward. An air conditioner unit on the north side of the home was lifted and transported fifteen feet to the east. ||Many trees were snapped along Mussetter Road. A large home suffered roof damage and structural damage and a barn was destroyed. |||A second residence also experienced an uplift of roofing. Several metal-sided barns were also destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front approached Maryland on the 29th. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front. Strong winds aloft combined with instability ahead of the front for some thunderstorms to become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to trees, telephone poles, and structures were surveyed near the intersection of Old Braddock Trail and Brookstree Lane. ||Dozens of trees were uprooted along Wagner Road as well as Oldtown Cemetery Road and Lower Town Creek Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road near the intersection of Route 214 and Beard's Point was closed due to flash flooding. Spotter reported 2.96 inches of rain near the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture streaming northeastward from an upper low over the Deep South with additional moisture from Tropical Storm Danny off the North Carolina coast pooled up against a stationary boundary near the Mason-Dixon Line. Several Rounds of heavy rain-producing showers and thunderstorms pushed eastward along this boundary from the early morning hours through the evening of the 28th. This produced flash flooding over northeast Maryland. Numerous reports of rainfall totals of 3 to 5 inches, with isolated reports of over 7 inches in southeastern Harford and north central Carroll counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 7 near Abingdon Road closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that moved through the Eastern Shore Friday afternoon helped produce strong to severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon of the 31st. Most of the wind damage occurred as a line of severe thunderstorms known as a bow echo moved across the region. Delmarva Power and Light reported about 8,000 homes and businesses lost power across the Delmarva Peninsula. Power was fully restored by August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in Rock Hall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west to northwest winds occurred across the Eastern Shore from the middle of the morning into the early evening on the 7th. Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph and knocked down some weak tree limbs. Measured wind gusts included 49 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport and 41 mph at Tolchester Beach (Kent County). The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between a low pressure system (around 990 millibars) that was moving northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system moving from the Central Plains toward the area.  As the high pressure system moved closer, winds diminished during the evening of the 7th.
